title: ALU implementation: rotate/shift (CB prefix)
description:
Implement CB-prefix rotate and shift operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- RLC r: rotate left circular, C=old bit 7, Z=result==0
- RRC r: rotate right circular, C=old bit 0, Z=result==0
- RL r: rotate left through carry, C=old bit 7, bit 0=old C, Z=result==0
- RR r: rotate right through carry, C=old bit 0, bit 7=old C, Z=result==0
- SLA r: shift left arithmetic, C=old bit 7, bit 0=0, Z=result==0
- SRA r: shift right arithmetic, C=old bit 0, bit 7 preserved, Z=result==0
- SRL r: shift right logical, C=old bit 0, bit 7=0, Z=result==0
- SWAP r: swap nibbles, Z=result==0, all other flags 0

Also: unprefixed rotate/shift (RLCA, RRCA, RLA, RRA):
- Same as RLC A, RRC A, RL A, RR A BUT Z flag is always 0 (not set on zero result!)
- This difference between prefixed and unprefixed rotates is a common bug.

title: ALU unit tests green (curated + generated vectors)
description:
Green test/unit/test_alu_generated_vectors.py and test/unit/test_alu.py.

Three verification lanes (§11.4):
1. Curated examples: hand-picked edge cases testing specific flag behaviors
   - ADD 0xFF + 0x01 = 0x00 (Z=1, C=1, H=1)
   - ADC 0x0F + 0x00 + carry=1 (H=1 from carry into high nibble)
   - SUB 0x00 - 0x01 (borrow, Z=0, N=1, H=1, C=1)
   - INC 0xFF = 0x00 (Z=1, C unchanged!)
   - DAA after 0x09 + 0x01 = 0x10 (BCD correction)
   
2. Generated vectors: systematic sweep from gbxcule-derived reference
   - Every ADD A,x for x in representative set
   - Every ADC with carry=0 and carry=1
   - ADD SP,e8 for representative SP values and signed offsets
   - LD HL,SP+e8 with same cases

3. Nightly exhaustive (pytest marker for CI):
   - All ADD A,x for all x (256*256 = 65536 cases)
   - All ADC A,x with carry 0/1 (256*256*2 = 131072 cases)
   - All SUB x, all SBC with carry 0/1
   - All DAA inputs over (A:256, N:2, H:2, C:2) = 2048 cases

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone B): All ALU unit tests green.

title: ALU implementation: INC/DEC 8-bit
description:
Implement 8-bit INC/DEC in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- INC r: r + 1, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=half-carry, C=PRESERVED (not affected!)
- DEC r: r - 1, flags Z=result==0, N=1, H=half-borrow, C=PRESERVED

TRICKY: INC/DEC PRESERVE the carry flag (§11.3). This is a common bug source. The f_prev input carries the previous flags, and only Z/N/H are updated.

title: Epic: CPU Core Bring-Up — Wave A (Phase 3b)
description:
Implement the CPU phase machine and bring all Wave A custom ROMs to green at instruction-boundary differential against the PyBoy oracle.

Wave A ROMs (§17.1):
- LOADS_BASIC: 8-bit loads, 16-bit loads, memory reads/writes via register pairs
- ALU_FLAGS: all 8-bit ALU ops with flag verification
- ALU16_SP: 16-bit arithmetic, ADD SP,e8, LD HL,SP+e8
- FLOW_STACK: PUSH, POP, CALL, RET, JP, JR (conditional and unconditional)
- CB_BITOPS: all CB-prefix rotate/shift/bit/res/set operations
- MEM_RWB: memory read/write-back soak test
- ALU_LOOP: ALU stress/iteration test

Implementation scope:
- Fetch/execute phase machine using the Phase enum from §8.1
- Typed continuation-driven control flow (§8.2)
- cpu_core entity wrapping step_mcycle pure function (§7.3)
- Minimal bus fabric: ROM + WRAM + HRAM + dummy IO reads (Wave A from §12.4)
- Write-enable discipline (§9.4): registers update only on explicit writeback
- Formal checks: F low nibble invariant, m_ce=0 hold behavior
- Metamorphic tests (§16.5): untouched registers unchanged, branch-not-taken uses short cycle

This phase does NOT include timers, interrupts, DMA, MBC, or PPU.

RATIONALE: Wave A deliberately covers most of the CPU instruction set without requiring any peripherals. This is the wave-based staging strategy (§17, Risk 5 mitigation) that prevents peripheral work from obscuring CPU bugs. The cpu_core entity is NOT a pipeline (§4.2)—it's a state machine with explicit registers. The phase machine uses typed continuations to make illegal control states unrepresentable.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone C): All Wave A ROMs green at instruction-boundary differential. No unexplained PC/register divergence. F low nibble invariant green.

title: Green ALU_LOOP stress ROM
description:
Write the ALU_LOOP stress test ROM and green it.

Purpose: extended ALU computation loops to stress the CPU over many instruction cycles.

Test approach:
- Fibonacci-style computation loop with register accumulation
- CRC-like computation using XOR/shift chains
- Counter loops with conditional branches
- Verify final register state against known values

This exercises the CPU over thousands of instructions, catching subtle state corruption that single-instruction tests miss.

Oracle mode: checkpoint (compare at loop endpoints, not every instruction)

title: Epic: Test Harness Infrastructure (Phase 1)
description:
Build the entire verification harness before writing real HDL. This is the plan's most counterintuitive but most important sequencing decision.

Covers:
- PyBoy oracle interface (§14.1): reset, step_commit, read_mem, write_event, snapshot, restore
- Hook-driven adapter for strict lockstep (§14.3): symbol lookup, instruction-boundary hooks, M-cycle stepping
- Replay capsule format (§16.7): ROM id, model/reset profile, seed, event log, symbols hash, first-divergence metadata
- Deterministic perturbation engine (§16.5.1): seeded event scripts keyed by commit index, not wall-clock time
- Simulation-only stimulus ports (§12.6: SimStimulus record)
- Divergence artifact framework: first bad CommitTrace, commit window, minimized replay capsule, localized waveform
- Scaffolded test files: decode completeness, ALU generated vectors, reset profiles, event determinism
- Placeholder lockstep test as xfail(strict=True)

RATIONALE: Thesis #2 says harness first. The plan explicitly warns (§16.1) against 'everything fails' and instead prescribes sanity-green meta tests proving generators/fixtures work, plus red semantic tests proving HDL isn't correct yet. Building the harness first means that when HDL lands in Phase 2, tests immediately fail on VALUE mismatches, not infrastructure errors. This is the difference between a harness that catches bugs and one that hides them.

The oracle is PyBoy-only (thesis #6). No second CPU emulator. No file-based golden traces (thesis #7). The replay capsule format provides reproducibility without trace files—it records the minimal information needed to re-derive any execution.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone A): Generators run reproducibly. Smoke scripts work from clean checkout. Harness tests green. HDL tests fail semantically, not infrastructurally.

title: Green MBC1_SWITCH and MBC1_RAM custom ROMs
description:
Write and green MBC1_SWITCH and MBC1_RAM test ROMs.

MBC1_SWITCH:
- Switch between ROM banks and read expected data
- Verify bank 0→1 mapping
- Verify bank wrapping for banks 0x00, 0x20, 0x40, 0x60
- Verify upper bits work in ROM banking mode

MBC1_RAM:
- Enable external RAM
- Write and read back data across RAM banks
- Verify RAM bank switching in RAM mode
- Verify RAM is inaccessible when disabled (reads return 0xFF)

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: CommitTrace and DebugTrace type definitions
description:
Create src/cpu/debug.spade with trace types from §7.2.

CommitTrace — the versioned comparison boundary (simulation-only):
  enum CommitKind { MCycle, InstrCommit, InterruptAck, Checkpoint }
  struct CommitTrace {
    schema_version: uint<16>,
    kind: CommitKind,
    seq: uint<64>,
    pc_before: uint<16>,
    opcode: Option<uint<8>>,
    arch_after: CpuArchState,
    phase_after: Phase,
    bus_req: BusReq,
    bus_resp: BusResp,
    mem_touch: Option<MemTouch>,
    io_touch: Option<IoTouch>,
  }
  struct MemTouch { kind: MemTouchKind, addr: uint<16>, data: uint<8> }
  struct IoTouch { addr: uint<16>, data: uint<8> }

DebugTrace — narrow internal debug record:
  (Define a minimal internal debug record for hardware-optional debug)

Compare scopes (§7.3):
- Every commit: PC/SP/registers/IME/halt/phase/bus req/bus resp
- On memory touch: address/data/region
- At checkpoints: selected memory windows or signatures
- PPU tests: semantic objects first, full frame hash last

DESIGN CONSTRAINTS (§4.4):
- CommitTrace is simulation-only, exposed only through simulation/test tops
- DebugTrace is narrow, internal only
- Hardware tops are FREE of trace fanout
- Schema version allows CommitTrace format evolution without breaking replay capsules

title: Bus ownership arbitration (BusOwner)
description:
Implement bus ownership arbitration in src/bus/membus.spade.

BusOwner enum (§12.2.2):
  enum BusOwner { Cpu, OamDma, Ppu, Idle }

Arbitration rules:
- Default owner: Cpu
- During OAM DMA: OamDma (CPU restricted to HRAM)
- During PPU pixel transfer: Ppu owns VRAM (CPU VRAM reads return 0xFF)
- During PPU OAM search: Ppu owns OAM (CPU OAM reads return 0xFF)

The CPU always emits plain BusReq. Contention is resolved in the fabric:
- If CPU requests a region owned by another master, the fabric returns 0xFF (DmgConservative) and the write is ignored
- BusObs sideband records the blocked access for diagnostics

This is separate from the CPU because bus ownership is a system-level concern (§12.2.2).

title: Replay capsule format definition
description:
Define the replay capsule format (§16.7) in bench/pyboy/replay.py.

A replay capsule is the minimal information needed to reproduce any test execution:
- ROM id (from manifest)
- ModelProfile
- ResetProfile
- MemoryBehaviorProfile
- Random seed (for generated action sequences)
- Injected event log (list of (commit_index, SimEvent) pairs)
- Symbol/hook manifest identity (hash of .sym file + hook config)
- First-divergence metadata (commit index, field, expected vs actual)

The capsule provides reproducibility WITHOUT file-based golden traces (thesis #7). Any engineer can re-derive the full execution from the capsule by:
1. Loading the ROM
2. Configuring the profiles
3. Replaying the event log with the same seed
4. Running to the divergence point

Capsules should be serializable to JSON for human readability and YAML for manifest integration.

title: Green ALU16_SP custom ROM
description:
Write the ALU16_SP custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- ADD HL,BC/DE/HL/SP with half-carry and carry edge cases
- INC/DEC rr (no flag changes)
- ADD SP,e8 with positive and negative offsets, verifying low-byte flag behavior
- LD HL,SP+e8 with same cases

Critical edge cases:
- ADD SP,1 with SP=0x00FF (half-carry from bit 3, carry from bit 7 of LOW byte)
- ADD SP,-1 with SP=0x0000
- ADD HL,rr where bit 11 carry occurs
- INC/DEC rr across 0x0000/0xFFFF boundary (no flags affected)

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Green EI_DELAY custom ROM
description:
Write and green the EI_DELAY test ROM.

Test coverage:
- EI followed by NOP: interrupts enabled after NOP, not during
- EI followed by HALT: interrupts enabled, then halt waits
- EI followed by DI: DI takes effect, canceling the pending enable
- Multiple EI instructions: still only delayed by one instruction
- EI followed by an instruction that should complete before interrupt service

Verification approach:
- Set up timer to fire interrupt at known time
- Execute EI at precise timing relative to pending interrupt
- Verify interrupt is NOT serviced during the instruction immediately after EI
- Verify interrupt IS serviced starting from the second instruction after EI

This tests the ImeState::PendingEnable → Enabled transition timing exactly.

Oracle mode: mcycle_commit (timing must be exact)

title: OAM DMA controller
description:
Implement OAM DMA controller in src/periph/ or src/bus/.

DMA behavior:
- Writing to 0xFF46 starts DMA: copies 160 bytes from source (written_value * 0x100) to OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F)
- Transfer takes 160 M-cycles
- During DMA, CPU can only access HRAM (0xFF80-0xFFFE) and IO registers
- All other memory reads return 0xFF (or bus conflict value per MemoryBehaviorProfile)

Implementation:
- DMA state machine entity with source address counter
- BusOwner::OamDma takes bus ownership during transfer
- Bus fabric routes DMA reads to source, DMA writes to OAM
- CPU reads during DMA are restricted (handled in bus fabric, NOT in CPU)

The CPU doesn't need to know about DMA restrictions — the bus fabric handles it (§12.2.2).

title: MBC3 mapper (ROM/RAM bank switching + RTC)
description:
Implement MBC3 memory bank controller.

MBC3 registers:
- 0x0000-0x1FFF: RAM/RTC Enable
- 0x2000-0x3FFF: ROM Bank Number (7 bits, 0 maps to 1)
- 0x4000-0x5FFF: RAM Bank / RTC Register Select (0x00-0x03: RAM, 0x08-0x0C: RTC)
- 0x6000-0x7FFF: Latch Clock Data (write 0x00 then 0x01 to latch)

RTC registers (optional, can stub initially):
- 0x08: Seconds, 0x09: Minutes, 0x0A: Hours, 0x0B: Day low, 0x0C: Day high + flags

Address mapping:
- 0x0000-0x3FFF: ROM bank 0 (always)
- 0x4000-0x7FFF: ROM bank N (7-bit, 1-127)
- 0xA000-0xBFFF: RAM bank or RTC register

For initial bring-up, RTC can return fixed values. Focus on ROM/RAM banking.

title: Phase machine for stack and control flow (PUSH, POP, JP, JR, CALL, RET, RST)
description:
Implement phase machine for stack operations and control flow.

Stack operations:
- PUSH rr: 4 M-cycles (fetch + internal + write high + write low). SP decremented first.
- POP rr: 3 M-cycles (fetch + read low + read high). SP incremented after each read.
  POP AF: low nibble of F is masked to zero (§9.3).

Unconditional jumps:
- JP nn: 4 M-cycles (fetch + read low + read high + internal)
- JP (HL): 1 M-cycle (fetch only, PC ← HL)
- JR e8: 3 M-cycles (fetch + read offset + internal branch)

Conditional jumps (cc = NZ, Z, NC, C):
- JP cc,nn: 3 M-cycles if not taken, 4 if taken
- JR cc,e8: 2 M-cycles if not taken, 3 if taken

Subroutine calls:
- CALL nn: 6 M-cycles (fetch + read low + read high + internal + push high PC + push low PC)
- CALL cc,nn: 3 if not taken, 6 if taken
- RST vec: 4 M-cycles (fetch + internal + push high + push low). Target = vec*8.

Returns:
- RET: 4 M-cycles (fetch + pop low + pop high + internal)
- RET cc: 2 if not taken, 5 if taken (extra internal cycle for condition check)
- RETI: like RET but also sets IME=Enabled

METAMORPHIC TEST (§16.5): Branch not taken MUST use the short cycle tuple.

title: Sprite rendering
description:
Implement sprite (OBJ) rendering.

OAM scan (Mode 2):
- Scan all 40 OAM entries
- Select up to 10 sprites visible on current scanline
- Sprite visible if: Y-16 <= LY < Y-16+height (height = 8 or 16 per LCDC bit 2)

Sprite attributes (4 bytes per entry):
- Byte 0: Y position (on-screen Y = stored_Y - 16)
- Byte 1: X position (on-screen X = stored_X - 8)
- Byte 2: Tile index (for 8x16 mode, bit 0 ignored)
- Byte 3: Flags
  - Bit 7: BG priority (0=sprite above BG, 1=sprite behind non-zero BG)
  - Bit 6: Y flip
  - Bit 5: X flip
  - Bit 4: Palette (0=OBP0, 1=OBP1)

Rendering priority:
- Sprites with lower X position have higher priority
- If same X, lower OAM index has higher priority
- Color 0 in sprite is always transparent

8x16 mode: tile index bit 0 forced to 0, top tile = index, bottom tile = index+1.

title: Constrained synth_ice40 + nextpnr baseline
description:
Run initial synthesis and place-and-route to establish baselines (§18.7 tier 3).

Steps:
1. Run synth_ice40 with explicit clock constraints
2. Run nextpnr-ice40 with the icebreaker.pcf pin file
3. Record baseline metrics:
   - LUT count
   - DFF count
   - SPRAM usage (4 blocks available on UP5K)
   - EBR usage (30 blocks available on UP5K)
   - Estimated f_max under clock constraint
   - Clock-enable fanout
4. Verify no debug-path contamination: hardware build should NOT include CommitTrace/DebugTrace fanout

The hardware synthesis top (src/board/icebreaker_top.spade) must be clean of simulation-only signals (§18.6).

Record results in a trackable format (JSON or markdown table) for regression comparison.

title: test_reset_profile.py scaffold
description:
Create test/harness/test_reset_profile.py (Phase 1 step 9).

This test verifies that the DUT's initial state after reset matches the expected post-boot state for the configured (ModelProfile, ResetProfile) pair.

For DMG + SkipBoot, verify:
- A=0x01, F=0xB0, B=0x00, C=0x13, D=0x00, E=0xD8, H=0x01, L=0x4D
- SP=0xFFFE, PC=0x0100
- IE=0x00, IF=0xE1 (typical)
- IME=disabled
- Timer registers at expected values
- Key IO registers at expected values

The test should:
1. Reset both DUT and oracle with the same profile
2. Compare initial register state
3. Optionally compare key memory locations

This is a 'sanity-green meta test' (§16.1) — it proves the reset infrastructure works, not that the CPU executes correctly.

title: cpu_test_top simulation top
description:
Create src/sim/cpu_test_top.spade — isolated CPU testing simulation top.

This top provides:
- Instantiates cpu_core with controllable inputs
- Mock/minimal bus: responds to reads from a small memory, accepts writes
- Controllable m_ce input for test-driven stepping
- Controllable irq_pending for interrupt injection
- Exposes CommitTrace outputs as flat Verilog-friendly ports for Cocotb
- SimStimulus input ports for deterministic test stimuli

Port naming: use stable, Verilog-friendly names (§4.4). Cocotb tests will reference these by name.

This is SEPARATE from the hardware synthesis top (§4.4, §18.6). Debug/trace fanout exists here, never in icebreaker_top.

title: test_alu_generated_vectors.py scaffold
description:
Create test/unit/test_alu_generated_vectors.py (§16.2, Phase 1 step 7).

This test generates ALU input/output vectors from a Python reference model (spec/flag_policies.py) and verifies the Spade ALU against them.

Three verification lanes (§11.4):
1. Curated examples: hand-picked edge cases for readability (inline in test)
2. Generated vectors: systematic sweep from gbxcule-derived flag formulas
3. Nightly exhaustive: selected small-domain full sweeps (behind pytest marker)

Initial scaffold should:
1. Define the Python reference ALU (spec/flag_policies.py)
2. Generate vectors for at least: ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, CP, INC, DEC
3. Parameterize tests with generated vectors
4. FAIL until ALU is implemented (red semantic tests)

Tricky flag cases to cover from §11.3:
- ADC/SBC: half-carry computed on nibble+carry, not nibble alone
- INC/DEC: preserves carry flag
- ADD SP,e8: zero/half-carry from low byte, not high byte
- LD HL,SP+e8: same flag behavior as ADD SP,e8
- DAA: depends on N, H, C flags AND accumulator value
- SCF/CCF: clear N and H, SCF sets carry, CCF inverts carry

title: cpu_core entity stub
description:
Create src/cpu/core.spade with the cpu_core entity stub from §6.2.

Interface (§6.2):
  entity cpu_core(
    clk: clock,
    rst: bool,
    m_ce: bool,
    bus_resp: BusResp,
    irq_pending: IrqPending,
  ) -> (BusReq, DebugTrace)

Implementation contract:
- Every architectural register uses reg(clk) with explicit hold behavior
- State changes happen ONLY when m_ce is true
- CPU can be simulated independently of hardware clock synthesis
- Returns (BusReq::Idle, default DebugTrace) as stub

Internal structure (§7.3):
- Registers store CpuState (arch + micro)
- step_mcycle is evaluated combinationally
- delta is committed on m_ce through field-local write enables
- apply_delta remains pure for tests and reference models

This entity is the operational realization of the denotational step_mcycle function. It is an ENTITY, not a pipeline (§4.2). The CPU is fundamentally a state machine with architecturally visible state and external bus interaction.

title: Create repository directory structure per §5 layout
description:
Create the full directory tree specified in §5 of the architecture plan. This is the physical organization that enforces the denotational/operational split.

Directories to create:
- spec/           — machine-readable opcode metadata, flag policies, compare scopes (Python)
- formal/         — SymbiYosys .sby files for formal verification
- tools/          — local smoke/regress/power/formal shell scripts
- toolchain/      — version pins and lockfiles for all external tools
- bench/manifests/ — ROM manifest YAML files
- bench/pyboy/    — oracle adapter: oracle.py, hooks.py, symbols.py, hook_driver.py, replay.py, checkpoints.py
- bench/actions/  — test action generators
- src/cpu/        — CPU semantic and operational code
- src/bus/        — bus types, memory fabric, address decode
- src/mem/logical/ — logical memory map
- src/mem/phys/   — physical memory primitives (EBR, SPRAM, inferred)
- src/periph/     — timer, serial, interrupts
- src/sim/        — simulation-only tops (cpu_test_top, soc_lockstep_top, inject_top, trace_top)
- src/board/      — hardware synthesis tops (clockgen, icebreaker_top)
- test/harness/   — harness infrastructure tests
- test/unit/      — isolated Spade block unit tests
- test/lockstep/  — live oracle lockstep tests
- test/rom/       — ROM-based integration tests
- test/ppu/       — PPU-specific tests
- test/power/     — power/activity tests

Move existing main.spade content: icebreaker_top goes to src/board/icebreaker_top.spade, update swim.toml source paths.

IMPORTANT: The sim/ vs board/ split is load-bearing for power (§18.6). Debug/trace fanout must NEVER be in hardware tops by default.

title: Green TIMER_DIV_BASIC custom ROM
description:
Write and green the TIMER_DIV_BASIC test ROM.

Test coverage:
- DIV increments at correct rate (every 256 T-cycles = 64 M-cycles for DMG)
- Writing to DIV resets the internal 16-bit counter to 0
- TIMA increments at rate selected by TAC clock select
- TAC enable/disable controls TIMA counting
- TIMA overflow triggers reload from TMA
- TIMA overflow sets IF bit 2
- Timer reload timing (1 M-cycle delay after overflow)

Edge cases:
- DIV write causing TIMA increment (falling-edge detection)
- TAC clock select change causing spurious increment
- Writing TIMA during the reload delay cycle cancels reload

Oracle mode: instr_commit or mcycle_commit for timing-critical tests

title: Epic: PPU Integration — Wave D (Phase 4.6)
description:
Implement the Picture Processing Unit (PPU) and bring Wave D ROMs to green.

Wave D0 — PPU timing/interrupt (§17.4):
- PPU mode state machine (OAM search → pixel transfer → HBlank → VBlank)
- STAT interrupt generation (mode transitions, LY=LYC coincidence)
- LCDC, STAT, LY, LYC, SCX, SCY, WX, WY, BGP, OBP0, OBP1 registers

Wave D1 — render semantics/visuals (§17.4):
- Background rendering: tilemap fetch, tile data lookup, palette application
- Scrolling: SCX/SCY with signed wrap
- Window rendering: WX/WY positioning
- Sprite rendering: OAM scan, priority, flip, palette

Memory regions:
- VRAM (0x8000-0x9FFF): tile data + tilemaps
- OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F): sprite attribute table

Verification approach (§17.4):
- Semantic checks: tilemap/window/sprite state queries, NOT just frame hashes
- PPU_STAT_IRQ: STAT interrupt timing and mode transitions
- BG_STATIC: static background rendering
- BG_SCROLL_SIGNED: scrolled background with signed offset arithmetic
- PPU_WINDOW: window overlay rendering
- PPU_SPRITES: sprite rendering with priority/flip/palette

RATIONALE: PPU is deliberately last in the CPU-first plan. It has the most complex timing interactions and the largest state space. By the time we reach Wave D, the CPU, timer, interrupt, and bus infrastructure are all verified, so PPU bugs can be isolated to PPU logic rather than being masked by CPU/timer issues (Risk 5 mitigation).

ACCEPTANCE: All Wave D ROMs green. PPU mode timing matches oracle. Semantic sprite/tile checks pass before frame hash verification.

title: HALT implementation
description:
Implement HALT behavior in the CPU phase machine.

States (§8.3):
  enum HaltState {
    Running,          // Normal execution
    Halted,           // CPU halted, waiting for interrupt
    HaltBugPending,   // HALT bug: next byte read twice (defer implementation)
  }

HALT behavior:
- When HALT executed: transition to HaltState::Halted
- In Halted state: CPU emits BusReq::Idle, no register writes, no ALU activity (§18.5)
- Wake condition: (IE & IF) != 0 (any enabled interrupt is pending)
- If IME=Enabled AND wake: service interrupt (transition to ServiceInterrupt phase)
- If IME=Disabled AND wake: resume execution at PC (no interrupt service)

HALT bug (defer to later):
- If IME=Disabled AND (IE & IF)==0 when HALT executes: HALT bug triggered
- Next instruction's first byte is read twice (PC doesn't increment on first read)
- HaltBugPending type exists now but implementation can be stubbed

POWER (§18.5): HALT must mean REAL stillness:
- No register writes
- No bus requests
- No ALU activity
- Stable phase/debug outputs in simulation

This is both a correctness requirement and a power architecture requirement.

title: Epic: HDL Type System & Skeleton (Phase 2)
description:
Create the compile-only Spade HDL skeleton: all types, stubs, and simulation tops that define the semantic boundaries of the CPU.

This phase produces NO working logic—only type definitions and stub entities that compile cleanly. The purpose is to:
1. Lock the type-level API contracts before implementation
2. Let Phase 1 tests run against stubs and fail on value mismatches, not build errors
3. Establish the denotational/operational split (§4.1) in code structure

Key type boundaries from §7.2:
- CpuArchState (regs, ime_state, halt_state)
- CpuMicroState (phase, opcode_latch, imm_lo/hi, decoded, temp)
- Phase enum with typed continuations (§8.1-8.2)
- CommitTrace with schema versioning (§7.2)
- BusReq/BusResp (§12.2) — CPU-facing, minimal
- BusObs/BusOwner (§12.2.1-12.2.2) — fabric-side, simulation-only
- AluReq with Idle case for operand isolation (§11.1-11.2)
- CpuDelta with sparse register writes (§7.2) — critical for power (§18.4)

Simulation tops:
- cpu_test_top: isolated CPU testing with controllable bus/interrupts
- soc_lockstep_top: full SoC for lockstep oracle comparison

RATIONALE: The Conal-style split (§4.1) is the architectural heart. Pure functions specify combinational semantics; entities own state. The type system encodes this split. Typed continuations (§8.2) make illegal phase transitions unrepresentable. The CpuDelta sparse-write pattern (§18.4) is designed-in from day one because power is an architectural property (thesis #3), not a cleanup pass.

ACCEPTANCE: All .spade files compile. Tests execute and fail on value mismatches, not build errors.

title: Physical memory mapping: SPRAM/EBR for large memories
description:
Verify and implement proper physical memory mapping (§18.7.1).

UP5K resources:
- 4 x SPRAM blocks (256Kbit each = 32KB each, single-port)
- 30 x EBR blocks (4Kbit each = 512B each, true dual-port)

Memory mapping plan:
- WRAM (8KB): 1 SPRAM block (uses 8KB of 32KB capacity) or 16 EBR blocks
- VRAM (8KB): 1 SPRAM block
- OAM (160 bytes): inferred registers or 1 EBR block
- HRAM (127 bytes): inferred registers
- Cartridge RAM: SPRAM or external

Implementation:
- src/mem/phys/spram.spade: SPRAM primitive wrapper
- src/mem/phys/ebr.spade: EBR primitive wrapper
- src/mem/logical/map.spade: logical-to-physical mapping

CRITICAL: Do not let large memories remain accidental register arrays in hardware builds. The clean wrapper boundary (§18.7.1) allows physical mapping changes without affecting CPU/bus semantics.

title: HALT quiescence verification test
description:
Create test/power/test_halt_quiescence.py.

Verify HALT means real stillness (§18.5):
1. Enter HALT state
2. Run for many M-cycles with no wake event
3. Verify on EVERY cycle:
   - No register writes (write-enable duty cycle = 0)
   - No bus requests (BusReq::Idle every cycle)
   - No ALU activity (AluReq::Idle every cycle)
   - Phase output is stable (Phase::Halted, no toggling)
   - Debug/trace outputs are stable (no toggling)

4. Then trigger a wake event (set IF bit with IE match)
5. Verify wake happens on the correct cycle
6. Verify interrupt service begins correctly

This is a POWER test (§18.5) — it validates that HALT is truly quiescent, which is critical for battery-optimized operation.

title: Pin PyBoy dependency with headless configuration
description:
Add PyBoy as a pinned Python dependency for the oracle/verification layer.

Requirements:
- Pin exact PyBoy version in toolchain/python.lock or requirements.txt
- Configure for headless mode: null window plugin, sound emulation off, unlimited speed (§14.2)
- Verify PyBoy can load a test ROM, step instructions, read registers, read/write memory
- Verify save/load checkpoint (snapshot/restore) works
- Document the specific PyBoy APIs we depend on in toolchain/versions.md

PyBoy is the SOLE behavioral oracle (thesis #6). We do NOT add a second CPU emulator. The hook-driven adapter (separate task) provides the deep control path for lockstep testing.

Run PyBoy in headless performance mode for all non-visual suites: null window, no input, sound emulation off, unlimited speed (§14.2).

title: Phase machine for CB-prefix operations
description:
Implement phase machine for CB-prefixed instructions.

CB prefix handling:
- Fetch 0xCB → Phase::FetchPrefix{prefix: Cb} → fetch next byte → decode_cb → execute

For register operands (B,C,D,E,H,L,A):
- 2 M-cycles: fetch CB + fetch opcode → execute ALU

For (HL) operand:
- BIT b,(HL): 3 M-cycles (fetch CB + fetch opcode + read (HL))
- RES/SET b,(HL): 4 M-cycles (fetch CB + fetch opcode + read (HL) + write (HL))
- Rotate/shift (HL): 4 M-cycles (fetch CB + fetch opcode + read (HL) + write (HL))

The continuation for CB (HL) operations is a ReadMem with the appropriate continuation for the CB operation type.

title: test_event_script_determinism.py scaffold
description:
Create test/harness/test_event_script_determinism.py (Phase 1 step 10).

This test verifies that the deterministic perturbation engine produces identical results across runs:
1. Generate an event script with seed S
2. Run the oracle with that script for N commits
3. Record the final state
4. Repeat with the same seed S
5. Assert identical final state

Also verify:
- Different seeds produce different event scripts
- Event scripts are keyed by commit index, not wall-clock time
- The same event stream applied to both DUT and oracle produces deterministic comparison results

This is another 'sanity-green meta test' (§16.1) — it validates the test infrastructure itself.

title: PPU registers (LCDC, SCX, SCY, WX, WY, BGP, OBP)
description:
Implement PPU control and palette registers.

LCDC (0xFF40) — LCD Control:
- Bit 7: LCD enable
- Bit 6: Window tilemap select (0=9800, 1=9C00)
- Bit 5: Window enable
- Bit 4: BG & Window tile data select (0=8800, 1=8000)
- Bit 3: BG tilemap select (0=9800, 1=9C00)
- Bit 2: OBJ size (0=8x8, 1=8x16)
- Bit 1: OBJ enable
- Bit 0: BG & Window enable/priority

Scroll registers:
- SCX (0xFF43): viewport X scroll
- SCY (0xFF42): viewport Y scroll
- WX (0xFF4B): window X position + 7
- WY (0xFF4A): window Y position

Palette registers:
- BGP (0xFF47): BG palette data (4 x 2-bit color indices)
- OBP0 (0xFF48): OBJ palette 0 (color 0 is transparent)
- OBP1 (0xFF49): OBJ palette 1 (color 0 is transparent)

title: Green deterministic interrupt injection scenarios
description:
Create and green test/lockstep/test_interrupt_injection.py.

Scenarios (§16.5):
1. Deterministic interrupt injection around EI: inject IF bits at precise commit indices relative to EI execution
2. Deterministic interrupt injection around HALT: inject at various points before/during/after HALT
3. Interrupt injection around RETI: verify no double-service
4. Multiple simultaneous interrupts: verify priority ordering
5. Interrupt during multi-M-cycle instruction: verify instruction completes first
6. halt-after-ei corner cases (§16.5)
7. Pending IF injection via SimStimulus if_set_bits/if_clear_bits

All stimuli use the deterministic perturbation engine: seeded event scripts keyed by commit index. Same stream applied to both DUT and oracle (§16.5.1).

title: ALU types: AluReq, AluResult
description:
Create src/cpu/alu.spade with ALU type definitions from §11.1 (stub implementation).

AluResult:
  struct AluResult { val8: uint<8>, val16: uint<16>, flags: Flags }

AluReq (§11.1) — exhaustive enum of all ALU operations:
  enum AluReq {
    Idle,                              // §11.2: operand isolation — inputs stay quiet
    Add8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>, carry_in: bool},
    Sub8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>, carry_in: bool},
    And8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Or8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Xor8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Cp8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Inc8{x: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Dec8{x: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Add16{a: uint<16>, b: uint<16>, z_preserve: bool},
    AddSpE8{sp: uint<16>, off: uint<8>},  // signed offset, tricky flags
    Daa{a: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Cpl{a: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Scf{f_prev: Flags},
    Ccf{f_prev: Flags},
    RotShift{...},                     // RLCA, RRCA, RLA, RRA, RLC, RRC, RL, RR, SLA, SRA, SRL, SWAP
    BitResSet{...},                    // BIT, RES, SET
  }

Stub implementation:
  fn alu(req: AluReq) -> AluResult  // Returns zeros for now

The Idle case is NOT aesthetics (§11.2). It ensures that when the CPU is not performing ALU work, the ALU input buses stay quiet and large adders do not see unrelated toggling. This is a power architecture decision.

title: Green ALU_FLAGS custom ROM
description:
Write the ALU_FLAGS custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP with various operand values
- Focus on flag edge cases: zero results, half-carries, carries, borrows
- INC/DEC with carry preservation verification
- DAA after ADD and SUB sequences
- CPL, SCF, CCF flag behavior

Each test:
1. Set up registers to known state
2. Execute ALU instruction
3. Check result register AND flag register
4. Write pass/fail to WRAM signature block
5. Jump to __fail if any check fails

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Create local smoke scripts for CI-free verification
description:
Create shell scripts in tools/ that run key verification steps locally. These must work from a clean checkout.

Scripts to create:
- tools/smoke.sh     — runs all fast checks: compile, unit tests, basic oracle init
- tools/regress.sh   — runs full regression: all test tiers
- tools/power.sh     — runs power-related checks: quiescence, activity capture
- tools/formal.sh    — runs formal verification suites
- tools/oracle.sh    — verifies PyBoy oracle: init, reset, symbol lookup, step, snapshot/restore

Each script should:
- Check for required tool versions
- Exit with clear error messages if tools are missing
- Support a --dry-run flag for CI integration testing
- Use devenv or cx for any missing tools per project conventions (CLAUDE.md)

These scripts are the operational definition of 'works from a clean checkout' (Milestone A acceptance criteria).

title: Epic: Bus Realism — Wave C (Phase 4.5)
description:
Implement realistic bus features: OAM DMA, memory banking (MBC1/MBC3), and bus ownership arbitration.

Components:
- OAM DMA controller: copies 160 bytes from source to OAM, CPU bus access restricted during transfer
- Bus ownership arbitration (§12.2.2): BusOwner enum {Cpu, OamDma, Ppu, Idle}
- DMA-era CPU accessibility restrictions per MemoryBehaviorProfile
- MBC1 mapper: ROM bank switching (5-bit bank), RAM bank switching (2-bit), mode select
- MBC3 mapper: ROM bank switching (7-bit bank), RAM bank switching + RTC registers

Wave C ROMs (§17.3):
- DMA_OAM_COPY: OAM DMA transfer correctness
- MBC1_SWITCH: ROM bank switching
- MBC1_RAM: external RAM enable/bank select
- MBC3_SWITCH: ROM bank switching with larger bank space
- MBC3_RAM: external RAM and RTC register access

RATIONALE: Bus realism is separated from CPU core (thesis: CPU should not directly know memory topology, §12.1). MBC mappers are the gateway to running real commercial ROMs. OAM DMA tests bus contention which is observable in real games. These are prerequisites for PPU work since PPU needs VRAM/OAM access arbitration.

ACCEPTANCE: All Wave C ROMs green. DMA transfer verified correct. Bank switching works for MBC1 and MBC3.

title: test_decode_completeness.py scaffold
description:
Create test/unit/test_decode_completeness.py (§16.2, Phase 1 step 6).

This test verifies that the decoder maps EVERY opcode to a valid DecodedOp:
- All 256 unprefixed opcodes (0x00-0xFF)
- All 256 CB-prefixed opcodes (0xCB00-0xCBFF)
- No opcode maps to an 'unknown' or 'invalid' variant unless it genuinely is (like 0xD3, 0xDB, 0xDD, 0xE3, 0xE4, 0xEB, 0xEC, 0xED, 0xF4, 0xFC, 0xFD on SM83)

Initially this test should be a scaffold that:
1. Imports the opcode metadata from spec/sm83_opcodes.py
2. For each opcode, calls the decoder and checks the result
3. FAILS until the decoder is implemented (red semantic test, not infrastructure failure)

The test should be driven from the canonical opcode metadata (§8.0), not handwritten opcode lists.

title: Test epic
description: test

title: Interrupt service phase machine (ServiceInterrupt)
description:
Implement the interrupt service sequence as a first-class phase (§8.1).

Phase:
  Phase::ServiceInterrupt { irq: IrqSel, subphase: IrqPhase }

Interrupt service sequence (5 M-cycles total):
1. Internal delay (2 M-cycles of bus idle)
2. Push PC high byte to stack (SP-1)
3. Push PC low byte to stack (SP-2)
4. Set PC to interrupt vector address

Interrupt vectors:
- VBlank:   0x0040
- LCD STAT: 0x0048
- Timer:    0x0050
- Serial:   0x0058
- Joypad:   0x0060

During service:
- IME set to Disabled
- The corresponding IF bit is cleared (via IrqAck to peripheral layer)
- SP decremented by 2

Priority: lower bit number = higher priority. If multiple interrupts pending, service the lowest-numbered one first.

The IrqPhase subphase enum tracks progress through the multi-M-cycle service sequence.

title: Green BG_STATIC, BG_SCROLL_SIGNED, PPU_WINDOW, PPU_SPRITES ROMs
description:
Write and green the Wave D1 visual test ROMs (§17.4).

BG_STATIC:
- Load a known tile pattern and tilemap
- Verify rendered pixels match expected output
- Use semantic tilemap/tile data checks, NOT frame hash alone

BG_SCROLL_SIGNED:
- Test background scrolling with SCX/SCY
- Include signed offset arithmetic edge cases
- Verify wrap-around behavior

PPU_WINDOW:
- Test window rendering at various WX/WY positions
- Verify window overlays background correctly
- Test window enable/disable via LCDC bit 5

PPU_SPRITES:
- Test sprite rendering with various attributes
- Verify priority ordering (X position, OAM index)
- Test flip flags
- Test 8x16 sprite mode
- Test BG priority flag
- Test sprite transparency (color 0)

Oracle mode: frame_semantic first, then frame_hash as coarsest checksum (§17.4)

title: Epic: Timer & Interrupt System — Wave B (Phase 4)
description:
Implement timer, interrupt controller, and all interrupt-related CPU behaviors. Bring Wave B ROMs to green.

Timer (§12.4 Wave B):
- DIV register (free-running 16-bit counter, upper 8 bits readable, write resets to 0)
- TIMA/TMA/TAC: configurable timer with overflow interrupt
- Timer derives from shared timebase (§6.1.1), NOT private local counter
- Timer falling-edge detection for obscure increment timing

Interrupts:
- IE/IF registers live in peripheral/io side, NOT inside CPU (§12.3)
- CPU receives narrow pending vector, emits acknowledge
- IME state machine: Disabled → PendingEnable → Enabled (§8.3)
- EI delay: exactly one instruction later (§16.5 invariant)
- RETI: return + enable interrupts atomically
- ServiceInterrupt phase with proper architectural sequence (§8.1)
- HALT: Running/Halted/HaltBugPending (§8.3) — HaltBugPending can be deferred but type must accommodate it

Wave B ROMs (§17.2):
- TIMER_DIV_BASIC: DIV counting, reset-on-write, TAC prescaler selection
- EI_DELAY: EI enables interrupts one instruction late
- TIMER_IRQ_HALT: timer overflow fires IRQ, wakes from HALT

Additional verification:
- Deterministic interrupt injection scenarios (§16.5)
- halt-after-ei corner cases
- HALT wake behavior matches pending IE & IF
- Interrupt service consumes correct architectural sequence
- JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST: joypad input divergence with persistent state (requires SimStimulus joyp_buttons)

RATIONALE: Interrupts and timers are the second-highest source of subtle CPU bugs after ALU flags. The plan (§8.3) insists they be first-class phase states, not conditionals buried in fetch. Making IE/IF external to the CPU (§12.3) is a modularity decision that simplifies later PPU/timer integration. The shared timebase (§6.1.1) prevents timer/CPU/DMA blocks from drifting by one cycle.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone D): EI_DELAY and TIMER_IRQ_HALT green. Live instruction-commit differential green on Wave B. Strict M-cycle differential green on targeted subsets. Every interrupt source observed in at least one green test. HALT wake confirmed.

title: Register write-enable duty cycle measurement
description:
Implement always-on logical quiescence metrics (§18.7 tier 1).

Metrics to track:
1. Register write-enable duty cycle: for each register (A,F,B,C,D,E,H,L,SP,PC), count cycles where write-enable is asserted vs total cycles
2. Bus-active duty cycle: fraction of cycles where BusReq != Idle
3. ALU-active duty cycle: fraction of cycles where AluReq != Idle
4. Halt quiescence ratio: fraction of halted cycles with truly zero activity

Implementation:
- Add counters to simulation tops (NOT hardware tops!)
- Report duty cycles after each test run
- Flag anomalies: e.g., PC write-enable > 50% is suspicious, register write-enable during HALT is a bug

These metrics are the 'canary in the coal mine' for power regressions. Track them in CI.

title: Bus types: BusReq, BusResp, BusObs, BusOwner, BusRegion
Decode types: DecodedOp, opcode enums
description:
Create src/cpu/decode_types.spade with instruction decode output types.

DecodedOp should capture:
- Operation kind (load, alu, control flow, CB-prefix, misc)
- Source and destination operands (register, immediate, memory via register pair)
- ALU operation kind for ALU instructions
- Condition code for conditional operations (NZ, Z, NC, C)
- Addressing mode

The exact enum shape will be refined during decoder implementation, but the type should:
1. Cover all SM83 instruction families
2. Be driven by the canonical opcode metadata (§8.0)
3. Support the continuation-based phase machine (the decoder output feeds into Phase transitions)

This is a stub — the actual decode logic comes in Phase 3a.

title: Minimal bus fabric: ROM + WRAM + HRAM + dummy IO
description:
Implement the Wave A memory model (§12.4) in src/bus/.

Address map for CPU bring-up:
- 0x0000-0x7FFF: ROM (read-only, loaded from test ROM binary)
- 0xC000-0xDFFF: WRAM (8KB read/write)
- 0xFF80-0xFFFE: HRAM (127 bytes read/write)
- 0xFF00-0xFF7F: IO registers (return 0xFF for reads, ignore writes as default)
- Everything else: return 0xFF for reads, ignore writes

Implementation files:
- src/bus/membus.spade: top-level address decoder and bus multiplexer
- src/bus/rom.spade: ROM module (preloaded in simulation)
- src/bus/wram.spade: WRAM module
- src/bus/hram.spade: HRAM module

The bus responds in 1 M-cycle (no wait states for Wave A).
BusReq::Idle → BusResp{data: 0xFF} (or last value, doesn't matter since CPU shouldn't use it).

POWER (§18.3): When BusReq::Idle, keep chip-selects low, write strobes false, avoid recomputing address paths.

This is enough memory to run Wave A ROMs without timers, interrupts, DMA, or MBC.

title: Pin synthesis and formal verification tool versions
description:
Pin exact versions for synthesis, place-and-route, and formal verification tools.

Tools to pin:
- yosys (synthesis: synth_ice40 command)
- nextpnr-ice40 (place and route)
- icepack/iceprog (bitstream packing and upload)
- SymbiYosys (formal verification framework)

title: CPU type definitions: CpuArchState, CpuMicroState, Phase enum, continuations
Divergence artifact emission framework
description:
Implement the divergence artifact framework (§16.7) for when live oracle comparison fails.

When a CommitTrace comparison fails, emit:
1. The first bad CommitTrace (DUT vs oracle)
2. A commit window before/after the failure (configurable, default ±5)
3. A minimized replay capsule (see replay capsule task)
4. A localized waveform dump (VCD/FST) centered on the failure
5. Optional in-memory oracle state snapshot

Artifact output:
- Human-readable summary to stderr
- Machine-readable JSON to artifact directory
- Waveform file for waveform viewer (surfer, GTKWave)

This framework is critical for debugging. When a test fails, the engineer should be able to:
1. Read the summary to understand WHAT diverged
2. Load the replay capsule to reproduce it
3. Open the waveform to see the signal-level picture
4. Inspect the oracle snapshot to understand the expected state

title: Simulation-only stimulus ports and soc_lockstep_top scaffold

title: SAIF activity capture on representative ROM windows
description:
Implement nightly activity captures (§18.7 tier 2).

Setup:
- Select representative ROM windows: boot sequence, ALU-heavy loop, HALT-heavy idle, mixed game play
- Run simulation with SAIF (Switching Activity Interchange Format) capture enabled
- Record toggle counts per signal
- Compare against baseline

SAIF captures provide the ground truth for dynamic power estimation. They show exactly which signals toggle how often during real workloads.

Output:
- SAIF file per ROM window
- Summary report: top 20 highest-toggle signals
- Comparison against baseline (regression detection)

These should run in the nightly regression, not in the fast CI loop.

title: Define model, reset, and memory behavior profile enums
description:
Define the three orthogonal profile enums that parameterize ALL simulation and testing (§13).

ModelProfile (§13.1):
- DMG: original Game Boy
- CGB: Game Boy Color (future, but type must exist now)

ResetProfile (§13.1):
- SkipBoot: post-boot register/memory state, no boot ROM execution
- RawPowerOn: true power-on state (requires boot ROM)

MemoryBehaviorProfile (§13.1.1):
- DmgConservative: safe defaults for all ambiguous behaviors
- DmgRevisionSpecific: matches a specific DMG hardware revision

The MemoryBehaviorProfile fixes:
- Echo RAM mirroring policy (0xE000-0xFDFF mirrors 0xC000-0xDDFF)
- 0xFEA0-0xFEFF read behavior (prohibited region)
- Blocked VRAM/OAM read/write policy during PPU access
- DMA-era CPU accessibility restrictions

For CPU bring-up: pin ModelProfile::DMG and ResetProfile::SkipBoot. Record these in replay capsules and ROM manifests.

These profiles should be defined in BOTH:
1. Python (spec/compare_scopes.py or similar) for oracle/harness use
2. Spade (src/cpu/types.spade or similar) for HDL parameterization

RATIONALE: §13 calls this 'one of the highest-risk correctness traps.' If reset state is wrong, every test result is suspect. Making profiles explicit and recording them in replay capsules makes divergences diagnosable.

title: Green MEM_RWB soak/stress ROM
description:
Write the MEM_RWB soak test ROM and green it.

Purpose: stress-test memory read/write/readback across the address space accessible in Wave A.

Test approach:
- Write patterns to WRAM, read back, verify
- Write patterns to HRAM, read back, verify
- Test various access patterns: sequential, strided, pseudo-random (LFSR-driven)
- Verify read-after-write consistency
- Test boundary addresses (start/end of each region)
- Test that ROM reads return expected values

This is a soak/stress test — it runs many iterations to build confidence in memory infrastructure.

Oracle mode: instr_commit or checkpoint (lower frequency comparison)

title: Timebase entity: T-cycle and M-cycle enables
description:
Create src/board/clockgen.spade with the canonical architectural timebase entity from §6.1.1.

Three clocks (§6.1):
1. board clock: physical iCEBreaker input clock
2. T-cycle: Game Boy timing quantum
3. M-cycle: 4 T-cycles

Timebase entity outputs:
  entity timebase(clk: clock, rst: bool) -> TimebaseOut {
    t_ce: bool,       // T-cycle clock enable
    m_ce: bool,       // M-cycle clock enable (every 4th T-cycle)
    sys_counter: uint<N>,  // canonical system counter
    t_index: uint<2>,      // 0-3 within current M-cycle
    m_index: uint<N>,      // M-cycle count
  }

DESIGN PRINCIPLES:
- CPU commits on m_ce, but cycle-sensitive devices derive edge timing from this shared source (§6.1.1)
- Timer and DIV derive from this shared counter, NOT private local counters
- This prevents timer, CPU, and DMA blocks from drifting by one cycle
- First milestone: m_ce can be asserted every N simulation clocks or even every clock (§6.5)
- Do NOT hardcode exact real-time frequency in first CPU milestone — logical correctness first
- Do NOT use fabric-gated clocks (§6.4) — single root clock + enables
- Harness may accelerate/decelerate m_ce but may NOT skip/synthesize architectural counter states

title: Green LOADS_BASIC custom ROM
description:
Write the LOADS_BASIC custom test ROM and green it against the DUT.

Test coverage:
- LD r,r for all register combinations
- LD r,n (immediate loads)

- LD A,(BC), LD A,(DE), LD (BC),A, LD (DE),A

ROM ABI (§17.0.1):
