title: Deterministic perturbation engine
description:
Implement the seeded deterministic perturbation engine (§16.5.1) in bench/actions/generators.py.

Core principle: ALL external stimuli are emitted from a seeded event script keyed by commit index or checkpoint symbol. The SAME event stream is applied to BOTH the DUT and the oracle. Events are NEVER keyed by wall-clock time.

Event types (matching SimStimulus from §12.6):
- Joypad button state changes (joyp_buttons)
- Interrupt flag set/clear (if_set_bits, if_clear_bits)
- IE override (ie_override)
- DMA start trigger (dma_start)
- Serial data injection (serial_inject)
- Architectural time freeze (freeze_arch_time)
- CPU-only hold (cpu_hold_only)

Generator interface:
- SeededEventScript: deterministic sequence from seed + ROM manifest
- Supports both hand-authored event lists and generated sequences
- Every event is (commit_index: int, event: SimEvent)

Do NOT use ad hoc hierarchical forcing (force/release) for ordinary test stimuli (§12.6). Reserve force/release for exceptional fault injection only.

title: Machine-readable opcode metadata source (sm83_opcodes.py)
description:
Create spec/sm83_opcodes.py — the single canonical opcode metadata source (§8.0).

This file is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for static instruction facts. It must contain:
- opcode byte (0x00-0xFF for unprefixed, 0x00-0xFF for CB-prefix)
- prefix class (none, CB)
- mnemonic (e.g., 'LD B,C', 'ADD A,(HL)', 'BIT 3,H')
- instruction length in bytes (1, 2, or 3)
- cycle tuple(s): (not-taken, taken) for conditional ops, single value otherwise
- flag policy: which flags are affected and how (Z, N, H, C: set/reset/affected/unchanged)
- operand and addressing forms
- default compare scope for differential testing

Format: Python data structure (list of dicts or dataclass instances) that can be:
1. Imported by test generators (test_decode_completeness.py, test_alu_generated_vectors.py)
2. Used to generate Spade lookup tables if needed
3. Used to drive coverage tracking across opcode families

Sources to cross-reference: Pan Docs, gbxcule templates, Gekkio's opcode tables.

The phase machine remains hand-authored (§8.0). Only static instruction facts come from this file.

title: ALU operand isolation (Idle case)
description:
Verify and enforce ALU operand isolation via AluReq::Idle (§11.2).

When AluReq::Idle:
- All ALU input operands should be zero/stable (not receiving toggling data)
- The ALU result should be a defined default (zeros)
- Large adders should not see unrelated toggling

Implementation:
- Ensure the match arm for Idle returns a known-zero AluResult
- Ensure the CPU control logic emits AluReq::Idle in all phases that don't need ALU computation
- Add a test that verifies Idle produces stable outputs

This is a POWER architecture decision (§11.2, §18.2), not aesthetics. In a real FPGA, toggling adder inputs wastes dynamic power even if the result is unused.

title: MBC1 mapper (ROM/RAM bank switching)
description:
Implement MBC1 memory bank controller.

MBC1 registers (memory-mapped, no reads):
- 0x0000-0x1FFF: RAM Enable (0x0A enables, other values disable)
- 0x2000-0x3FFF: ROM Bank Number (5 bits, bank 0 maps to 1)
- 0x4000-0x5FFF: RAM Bank Number / Upper ROM Bank (2 bits)
- 0x6000-0x7FFF: Banking Mode Select (0=ROM mode, 1=RAM mode)

Address mapping:
- 0x0000-0x3FFF: ROM bank 0 (or bank N*0x20 in RAM mode)
- 0x4000-0x7FFF: ROM bank N (5-bit + optional 2-bit upper)
- 0xA000-0xBFFF: External RAM (if enabled, 8KB per bank, up to 4 banks)

Special cases:
- ROM bank 0x00 maps to 0x01 (can never select bank 0 in upper region)
- Similarly 0x20→0x21, 0x40→0x41, 0x60→0x61
- RAM mode changes which ROM bank appears in 0x0000-0x3FFF

title: Phase machine for ALU instructions
description:
Implement phase machine for 8-bit ALU instruction families.

Instruction families:
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP A,r: single M-cycle, ALU op on register operand
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP A,n: fetch immediate, then ALU op
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP A,(HL): read from (HL), then ALU op
- INC/DEC r: single M-cycle with flag-preserving behavior
- INC/DEC (HL): read-modify-write at (HL) — read, ALU, write back
- DAA/CPL/SCF/CCF: single M-cycle, misc ALU ops on A

Phase transitions:
- Register-register: Fetch → Execute (1 M-cycle)
- Immediate: Fetch → ReadImm8{k: AluFromImm} → Execute (2 M-cycles)
- Memory: Fetch → ReadMem{addr: HL, k: AluFromMem{kind}} → Execute (2 M-cycles)
- INC/DEC (HL): Fetch → ReadMem → ALU → WriteMem → Done (3 M-cycles)

The ALU is invoked via AluReq — the phase machine selects the correct AluReq variant based on DecodedOp.

title: ALU implementation: DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF
description:
Implement miscellaneous ALU operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- DAA: Decimal Adjust Accumulator (BCD correction)
- CPL: Complement A (bitwise NOT), flags N=1, H=1, Z/C preserved
- SCF: Set Carry Flag, flags N=0, H=0, C=1, Z preserved
- CCF: Complement Carry Flag, flags N=0, H=0, C=!C, Z preserved

TRICKY: DAA (§11.3) is the single most complex ALU operation:
- Behavior depends on N flag (was last op add or subtract?)
- Behavior depends on H flag and C flag from previous operation
- Behavior depends on current value of A
- After ADD/ADC (N=0): if A>0x99 or C, add 0x60 and set C; if (A&0xF)>9 or H, add 0x06
- After SUB/SBC (N=1): if C, subtract 0x60; if H, subtract 0x06
- Z flag set if result==0, N preserved, H=0, C set if correction overflowed

Do NOT re-derive DAA from memory. Translate the exact gbxcule formula.

title: ALU implementation: 8-bit arithmetic (ADD, ADC, SUB, SBC, CP)
description:
Implement 8-bit arithmetic operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- ADD A,r/n: A + operand, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=half-carry, C=carry
- ADC A,r/n: A + operand + carry_in, same flags but H/C include carry_in
- SUB r/n: A - operand, flags Z=result==0, N=1, H=half-borrow, C=borrow
- SBC A,r/n: A - operand - carry_in, same flags but H/C include carry_in
- CP r/n: same as SUB but result discarded, only flags set

TRICKY FLAG CASES (§11.3, lift from gbxcule):
- ADC half-carry: (A & 0xF) + (operand & 0xF) + carry_in > 0xF
- ADC carry: A + operand + carry_in > 0xFF (use wider arithmetic)
- SBC half-borrow: (A & 0xF) - (operand & 0xF) - carry_in < 0 (signed)
- SBC borrow: A - operand - carry_in < 0

Do NOT re-derive from memory. Translate actual gbxcule formulas.
Verify with generated vectors from spec/flag_policies.py.

title: Placeholder lockstep test as xfail
description:
Create test/lockstep/test_cpu_lockstep.py as xfail(strict=True) (Phase 1 step 11).

This test will eventually run live CPU lockstep comparison between the Spade DUT and the PyBoy oracle. Initially it should:
1. Exist as a proper test file
2. Be marked xfail(strict=True) — it MUST fail, and if it accidentally passes, that's a test error
3. Attempt to: load a simple ROM, reset both DUT and oracle, step one instruction, compare CommitTrace
4. Fail because the HDL stub doesn't produce correct values

The xfail is removed in Phase 4 when the private step adapter is ready (Phase 4 step 32).

IMPORTANT (§16.6): Keep the live oracle interface and replay capsule format stable from day one. If strict M-cycle stepping isn't wired up yet, gate only instruction-boundary suites. Do NOT introduce a file-trace fallback as a temporary workaround.

title: Green CB_BITOPS custom ROM
description:
Write the CB_BITOPS custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- RLC/RRC/RL/RR for representative registers and values
- SLA/SRA/SRL for edge cases (MSB/LSB behavior)
- SWAP for various values
- BIT b,r for all bit positions (verify Z flag = !tested_bit, H=1, N=0)
- RES b,r and SET b,r (verify no flag changes, correct bit manipulation)
- Operations on (HL): read-modify-write with correct timing

Edge cases:
- Rotate through carry with carry=0 and carry=1
- SRA preserves MSB (arithmetic shift)
- SRL clears MSB (logical shift)
- SWAP of 0x00 sets Z flag

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Background and window rendering
description:
Implement background and window pixel rendering.

Background rendering:
1. For each pixel on the current scanline:
2. Compute tilemap coordinates: ((SCY + LY) / 8, (SCX + pixel_x) / 8)
3. Look up tile index from selected tilemap (LCDC bit 3)
4. Fetch tile data from selected tile data area (LCDC bit 4)
5. Extract pixel from tile row using sub-tile coordinates
6. Apply BGP palette

Window rendering:
1. Window is active when LCDC bit 5 is set AND WY <= LY AND WX-7 <= pixel_x
2. Window uses its own internal line counter (doesn't use SCX/SCY)
3. Window tilemap selected by LCDC bit 6
4. Window overlays background (higher priority)

Tile data format:
- Each tile is 8x8 pixels, 2 bits per pixel
- 16 bytes per tile: 2 bytes per row, interleaved bit planes
- Byte 0 = low bits, Byte 1 = high bits for row 0, etc.

LCDC bit 4 tile data addressing:
- 1: unsigned, 0x8000 base (tiles 0-255)
- 0: signed, 0x9000 base (tiles -128 to 127, where tile 0 = 0x9000)

title: Phase machine for 16-bit arithmetic
description:
Implement phase machine for 16-bit arithmetic instructions.

Instructions:
- ADD HL,rr: 2 M-cycles (fetch + internal ALU with extra cycle for 16-bit add)
- INC rr: 2 M-cycles (fetch + internal)
- DEC rr: 2 M-cycles (fetch + internal)
- ADD SP,e8: 4 M-cycles (fetch + read imm + internal + internal)
- LD HL,SP+e8: 3 M-cycles (fetch + read imm + internal)

16-bit INC/DEC do NOT affect flags (unlike 8-bit INC/DEC).
ADD HL,rr affects N, H, C but preserves Z.
ADD SP,e8 and LD HL,SP+e8 have the tricky low-byte flag behavior (see ALU task).

title: IME state machine implementation
description:
Implement the IME (Interrupt Master Enable) state machine in the CPU.

States (§8.3):
  enum ImeState {
    Disabled,       // Interrupts disabled (after DI or reset)
    PendingEnable,  // EI executed, will enable AFTER next instruction
    Enabled,        // Interrupts enabled (can be serviced)
  }

Transitions:
- DI → ImeState::Disabled (immediate)
- EI → ImeState::PendingEnable (NOT immediate! Delayed by one instruction)
- After executing the instruction following EI: PendingEnable → Enabled
- Interrupt service entry → Disabled (interrupts disabled during service)
- RETI → Enabled (immediate, unlike EI)

The EI delay (§16.5 invariant): EI enables interrupts exactly one instruction later. This is critical for the common pattern:
  EI
  HALT  ; interrupts become enabled, then HALT waits for interrupt

If EI took effect immediately, the interrupt could fire before HALT executes.

The PendingEnable state is checked during the fetch phase: if an interrupt is pending AND IME is Enabled (not PendingEnable), service the interrupt instead of fetching the next instruction.

title: Define ROM manifest schema and initial inventory
description:
Create the ROM manifest schema (§17.0) and initial inventory of custom ROMs needed for Wave A-D.

Schema (bench/manifests/rom_schema.yaml or similar):
- id: unique ROM identifier
- path: relative path to ROM binary
- requires: [cpu, timer, dma, mbc1, mbc3, ppu] — capability requirements
- oracle_mode: unit | instr_commit | mcycle_commit | checkpoint | frame_semantic | frame_hash
- memory_behavior_profile: DmgConservative | DmgRevisionSpecific
- timeout_commits: max commits before test timeout
- checkpoint_symbols: list of __checkpoint_* labels
- pass_condition: how to determine pass (e.g., __pass label hit, WRAM signature)
- compare_scope: what to compare at each commit boundary
- action_script: optional deterministic event script path
- action_gen: optional generator for randomized but seeded action sequences

Custom ROM checkpoint ABI (§17.0.1):
- Every custom ROM must emit .sym file
- Reserved executable labels: __pass, __fail, __checkpoint_*, __commit_*, __inject_begin_*, __inject_end_*
- Versioned WRAM signature block for pass/fail and debug counters

Initial inventory (stub entries, ROMs not yet written):
Wave A: LOADS_BASIC, ALU_FLAGS, ALU16_SP, FLOW_STACK, CB_BITOPS, MEM_RWB, ALU_LOOP
Wave B: TIMER_DIV_BASIC, EI_DELAY, TIMER_IRQ_HALT
Wave C: JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST, DMA_OAM_COPY, MBC1_SWITCH, MBC1_RAM, MBC3_SWITCH, MBC3_RAM
Wave D: PPU_STAT_IRQ, BG_STATIC, BG_SCROLL_SIGNED, PPU_WINDOW, PPU_SPRITES

title: Green PPU_STAT_IRQ custom ROM
description:
Write and green PPU_STAT_IRQ test ROM.

Test coverage:
- Mode transitions: verify correct mode sequence per scanline
- LY increments correctly through 0-153
- LY=LYC coincidence flag in STAT
- STAT interrupt on mode 0, 1, 2 transitions
- STAT interrupt on LY=LYC
- STAT interrupt source combinations
- VBlank interrupt (IF bit 0) on line 144

Oracle mode: mcycle_commit for timing, instr_commit for flag/register checks

title: Timer/DIV entity (DIV, TIMA, TMA, TAC)
description:
Implement the timer subsystem in src/periph/timer.spade.

Registers:
- DIV (0xFF04): upper 8 bits of 16-bit system counter. Writing ANY value resets to 0.
- TIMA (0xFF05): timer counter. Increments at rate selected by TAC. Overflows → interrupt + reload from TMA.
- TMA (0xFF06): timer modulo. Value loaded into TIMA on overflow.
- TAC (0xFF07): timer control. Bit 2 = enable. Bits 1-0 = clock select:
  - 00: CPU clock / 1024 (4096 Hz)
  - 01: CPU clock / 16 (262144 Hz)
  - 10: CPU clock / 64 (65536 Hz)
  - 11: CPU clock / 256 (16384 Hz)

CRITICAL DESIGN DECISION (§6.1.1):
Timer derives from the SHARED timebase entity's sys_counter, NOT a private local counter. This prevents timer, CPU, and DMA blocks from drifting by one cycle.

Timer increment uses falling-edge detection on the selected bit of the system counter. This is the source of obscure timer bugs:
- Writing 0 to DIV can cause TIMA to increment if the selected counter bit was 1
- Changing TAC clock select can cause spurious TIMA increment
- These are real hardware behaviors that test ROMs verify

Timer overflow sequence:
1. TIMA overflows (0xFF → 0x00)
2. One M-cycle delay
3. TIMA reloaded from TMA
4. IF bit 2 (timer) set
5. During the delay cycle, writing TIMA cancels the reload and interrupt

title: Decoder: unprefixed opcodes
description:
Implement unprefixed opcode decoder in src/cpu/decode.spade.

Decoder function:
  fn decode(opcode: uint<8>) -> DecodedOp

Must cover all 256 unprefixed opcodes (0x00-0xFF):
- 8-bit loads: LD r,r / LD r,n / LD r,(HL) / LD (HL),r / LD (HL),n / LD A,(rr) / LD (rr),A
- 16-bit loads: LD rr,nn / LD SP,HL / LD (nn),SP / PUSH rr / POP rr / LD HL,SP+e8
- 8-bit ALU: ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP with r/(HL)/n
- 16-bit arithmetic: ADD HL,rr / INC rr / DEC rr / ADD SP,e8
- 8-bit INC/DEC: INC r / DEC r / INC (HL) / DEC (HL)
- Rotate/shift (unprefixed): RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA
- Control flow: JP/JR/CALL/RET/RETI/RST (conditional and unconditional)
- Misc: NOP, HALT, STOP, DI, EI, DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF
- Invalid: 0xD3,0xDB,0xDD,0xE3,0xE4,0xEB,0xEC,0xED,0xF4,0xFC,0xFD

Drive the decode table from the canonical opcode metadata (spec/sm83_opcodes.py) to ensure completeness.

title: Decoder: CB-prefixed opcodes
description:
Implement CB-prefix decoder in src/cpu/decode.spade.

Function:
  fn decode_cb(opcode: uint<8>) -> DecodedOp

All 256 CB-prefixed opcodes follow a regular pattern:
- 0x00-0x07: RLC r (rotate left circular)
- 0x08-0x0F: RRC r (rotate right circular)
- 0x10-0x17: RL r (rotate left through carry)
- 0x18-0x1F: RR r (rotate right through carry)
- 0x20-0x27: SLA r (shift left arithmetic)
- 0x28-0x2F: SRA r (shift right arithmetic)
- 0x30-0x37: SWAP r (swap nibbles)
- 0x38-0x3F: SRL r (shift right logical)
- 0x40-0x7F: BIT b,r (test bit)
- 0x80-0xBF: RES b,r (reset bit)
- 0xC0-0xFF: SET b,r (set bit)

Register encoding in bits [2:0]: B,C,D,E,H,L,(HL),A
Bit index in bits [5:3] for BIT/RES/SET

The regular structure means decode_cb can be largely computed from bit slicing rather than a 256-entry lookup table.

title: Green DMA_OAM_COPY custom ROM
description:
Write and green the DMA_OAM_COPY test ROM.

Test coverage:
- Trigger DMA by writing source address to 0xFF46
- Verify all 160 bytes copied correctly from source to OAM
- Verify DMA takes correct number of M-cycles
- Verify CPU is restricted to HRAM during DMA
- Verify CPU can read/write HRAM normally during DMA
- Verify non-HRAM reads during DMA return expected values (0xFF per DmgConservative)

Oracle mode: mcycle_commit (timing-sensitive)

title: ALU exhaustive nightly sweeps
description:
Add nightly exhaustive ALU verification sweeps (§11.4 lane 3).

Behind a pytest marker (e.g., @pytest.mark.nightly or @pytest.mark.exhaustive):
- All ADD A,x: 256 * 256 = 65,536 input pairs
- All ADC A,x with carry 0 and carry 1: 256 * 256 * 2 = 131,072
- All SUB x: 256 * 256 = 65,536
- All SBC A,x with carry 0 and carry 1: 131,072
- All DAA inputs: A(256) * N(2) * H(2) * C(2) = 2,048

Each case compares Spade ALU output against Python reference model.
Total: ~400K+ test vectors per nightly run.

These should NOT be in the fast CI loop — they run in the nightly regression (tools/regress.sh with --nightly flag).

title: ALU implementation: BIT/RES/SET (CB prefix)
description:
Implement CB-prefix bit operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- BIT b,r: test bit b of register r. Z=!bit_b, N=0, H=1, C=preserved
- RES b,r: reset (clear) bit b of register r. No flag changes.
- SET b,r: set bit b of register r. No flag changes.

b ranges from 0-7, covering all 8 bits.
Each operation exists for all 8 registers (B,C,D,E,H,L,(HL),A) = 8*8*3 = 192 CB opcodes total for these three families.

BIT is the only one that affects flags. RES and SET are pure data operations.

title: Fetch/execute phase machine for load instructions
description:
Implement the Phase state machine in src/cpu/control.spade and src/cpu/semantics.spade for load instruction families.

Load families to implement:
- LD r,r: single M-cycle, no memory access
- LD r,n: fetch immediate byte (ReadImm8 → LoadReg8 continuation)
- LD r,(HL): read from memory at HL (ReadMem → LoadReg8)
- LD (HL),r: write register to memory at HL (WriteMem → StoreDone)
- LD (HL),n: fetch immediate, then write to (HL) — two M-cycles after fetch
- LD A,(rr): read from BC/DE/HL address
- LD (rr),A: write A to BC/DE/HL address
- LD A,(nn): fetch 16-bit immediate, then read from that address
- LD (nn),A: fetch 16-bit immediate, then write A to that address
- LD rr,nn: fetch 16-bit immediate into register pair
- LD SP,HL: copy HL to SP (1 extra M-cycle)
- LD (nn),SP: store SP to memory at nn (16-bit immediate address)
- LD HL,SP+e8: SP + signed immediate, special flags (shares ALU with ADD SP,e8)
- LDH A,(n): read from 0xFF00+n
- LDH (n),A: write A to 0xFF00+n
- LDH A,(C): read from 0xFF00+C
- LDH (C),A: write A to 0xFF00+C
- LD A,(HL+): load A from (HL), then increment HL
- LD A,(HL-): load A from (HL), then decrement HL
- LD (HL+),A: store A to (HL), then increment HL
- LD (HL-),A: store A to (HL), then decrement HL
- PUSH rr: decrement SP, write high byte, decrement SP, write low byte
- POP rr: read low byte, increment SP, read high byte, increment SP

The phase machine uses typed continuations (§8.2). Each memory access transitions through ReadMem/WriteMem with the appropriate continuation carrying the semantic intent.

fn step_mcycle drives the transition: given current Phase + bus response → next Phase + bus request + delta.

title: ALU implementation: 16-bit ADD, ADD SP,e8, LD HL,SP+e8
description:
Implement 16-bit arithmetic in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- ADD HL,rr: HL + rr, flags Z=PRESERVED, N=0, H=half-carry(bit 11), C=carry(bit 15)
- ADD SP,e8: SP + signed_extend(e8), flags Z=0, N=0, H=half-carry(bit 3!), C=carry(bit 7!)
- LD HL,SP+e8: same computation as ADD SP,e8, same flag behavior

TRICKY FLAG CASES (§11.3):
- ADD HL,rr: half-carry is from bit 11 (not bit 3), carry from bit 15 (not bit 7). Z flag is PRESERVED.
- ADD SP,e8: despite being 16-bit, H/C flags are computed from the LOW BYTE of SP + e8. Z is always 0. This is extremely counterintuitive.
- LD HL,SP+e8: SAME flag behavior as ADD SP,e8 (Z=0, N=0, H from low byte, C from low byte)

These are the most commonly mis-implemented ALU operations in Game Boy cores. Lift directly from gbxcule.

title: Metamorphic and invariant tests for CPU core
description:
Implement metamorphic and invariant tests from §16.5.

Tests to implement:
1. Untouched registers: registers not targeted by an instruction remain unchanged after execution
2. m_ce=0 hold: holding m_ce=0 leaves ALL architectural state unchanged (formal or simulation)
3. Branch not-taken cycle count: branch not taken uses the short cycle tuple
4. F low nibble: always zero (simulation complement to formal check)
5. Memory write timing: writes occur only in the documented M-cycle
6. cpu_hold_only: freezing CPU leaves timer/peripheral time unchanged
7. freeze_arch_time: freezing arch time leaves CPU+peripheral arch time unchanged

Implementation:
- Python/Cocotb tests in test/unit/test_cpu_invariants.py
- Each test is parameterized over instruction families
- Use the canonical opcode metadata to drive which registers each instruction should/shouldn't touch

These are the tests that catch 'action at a distance' bugs — the kind where fixing one instruction subtly breaks another.

title: Green JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST custom ROM
description:
Write and green the JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST test ROM.

Purpose: test joypad input with persistent state divergence between DUT and oracle, verifying that the deterministic stimulus system keeps both in sync.

Test approach:
1. Configure joypad interrupt
2. Use SimStimulus joyp_buttons to inject button presses at specific commit indices
3. Verify joypad register (0xFF00) reads correct values
4. Verify joypad interrupt fires when buttons pressed
5. Verify state remains consistent after button release

This ROM tests the SimStimulus → DUT path AND the equivalent event injection into the PyBoy oracle. Both must see the same button events at the same logical time.

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: RETI implementation
description:
Implement RETI instruction in the CPU phase machine.

RETI = RET + set IME to Enabled (immediately, unlike EI).

Sequence (4 M-cycles, same as RET):
1. Fetch RETI opcode
2. Pop low byte of return address from stack
3. Pop high byte of return address from stack
4. Internal cycle, set PC to popped address, set IME=Enabled

Unlike EI, RETI enables interrupts IMMEDIATELY (no one-instruction delay). This is because RETI is typically the last instruction of an interrupt handler, and the next instruction will be in the interrupted code which should already be interruptible.

RETI is the normal way to exit an interrupt handler. Using RET from an interrupt handler would leave IME=Disabled, causing interrupts to stop working.

title: PyBoy oracle interface scaffold
description:
Implement the stable live oracle interface (§14.1) in bench/pyboy/oracle.py.

Interface (Python class or protocol):
  class Oracle:
    def reset(model_profile, reset_profile) -> None
    def step_commit() -> OracleCommit
    def read_mem(addr: int) -> int
    def write_event(ev: SimEvent) -> None
    def snapshot() -> bytes
    def restore(snapshot: bytes) -> None

OracleCommit should map to the CommitTrace schema (§7.2) — at minimum: pc_before, opcode, register state after, phase after, bus request, bus response.

Implementation details:
- Wrap PyBoy instance in headless mode
- step_commit() calls pyboy.tick() and extracts post-state
- read_mem() uses pyboy.memory[] or equivalent API
- snapshot()/restore() use PyBoy save state mechanism
- Must be deterministic: same ROM + same events = same trace

This is the ONLY oracle (thesis #6). No second emulator. No file-based golden traces (thesis #7).

title: soc_lockstep_top simulation top
description:
Create src/sim/soc_lockstep_top.spade — full SoC lockstep simulation top.

This top provides:
- Instantiates cpu_core + timebase + bus fabric + memory
- Full system composition for lockstep oracle comparison
- SimStimulus input ports (§12.6)
- CommitTrace outputs as flat ports for Cocotb
- BusObs sideband output for memory access diagnostics

Unlike cpu_test_top, this one composes the full system and is used for ROM-based integration tests and live oracle lockstep.

Initially instantiates only Wave A memory model: ROM + WRAM + HRAM + dummy IO. Expanded in later waves.

title: Encoding optimization evaluation
description:
Evaluate encoding optimizations based on measurement (§18.8).

Candidates:
- Phase enum: binary vs one-hot encoding
- BusReq: binary vs one-hot
- AluReq: binary vs one-hot
- Register select: binary vs one-hot
- State machine encodings

Process:
1. Measure toggle counts from SAIF activity captures
2. Compare area (LUTs) and toggle counts for different encodings
3. Only change encodings where measurement shows significant benefit
4. Re-verify correctness after any encoding change

IMPORTANT (§18.8): Binary enums, one-hot, and Gray encodings all trade area and toggles differently on FPGA fabrics. Let measurement drive encoding decisions. The first pass should prioritize clarity and typed structure.

title: Pipeline insertion evaluation
description:
Evaluate pipeline insertion candidates based on synthesis timing reports (§19).

Candidates (§19.3):
1. decode → operand select → ALU → flag pack (main CPU datapath)
2. pixel fetch → shade/merge → output (PPU)
3. cartridge/PPU address pipelines

Evaluation criteria:
- Does the combinational path exceed the target f_max?
- How much area does pipeline insertion cost (extra registers)?
- Does pipeline insertion change the semantic interface?

Process (§19.4):
1. Run synthesis/P&R with timing analysis
2. Identify critical paths
3. If critical path is in a candidate cluster: wrap in pipeline(N)
4. Re-verify with formal equivalence (formal/cpu_refactor.eqy) or differential tests
5. Update CPU phase machine if execute latency changes (§19.5)

IMPORTANT (§19.0): Do NOT use dynamic pipelines in CPU control core. Confine to leaf datapaths with clear request/response boundaries.

This task should only be started AFTER Milestone E baseline is recorded.

title: Epic: ALU & Decode Subsystems (Phase 3a)
description:
Implement and fully verify the ALU and instruction decoder as isolated, pure Spade functions before integrating them into the CPU core.

ALU (§11):
- Pure combinational function: fn alu(req: AluReq) -> AluResult
- 8-bit arithmetic: ADD, ADC, SUB, SBC, CP with correct flag computation
- 8-bit logic: AND, OR, XOR with correct flag computation
- INC/DEC 8-bit with flag preservation rules
- 16-bit: ADD HL,rr / ADD SP,e8 / LD HL,SP+e8 (tricky flag cases from gbxcule)
- Miscellaneous: DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF
- CB-prefix: all rotate/shift variants, BIT/RES/SET
- Operand isolation: Idle case ensures ALU inputs stay quiet when unused (§11.2)
- Three verification lanes (§11.4): curated examples, generated vectors from gbxcule, nightly exhaustive sweeps

Decoder (§8.0):
- Machine-readable opcode metadata source (sm83_opcodes.py): opcode, prefix, mnemonic, length, cycles, flag policy, operands, compare scope
- Unprefixed opcode decode: maps opcode byte to DecodedOp
- CB-prefixed decode: maps CB opcode byte to DecodedOp
- Decode completeness test: every opcode maps to something, no holes

RATIONALE: These are the two largest pure-function subsystems. Verifying them in isolation (§16.2) removes semantic uncertainty before lockstep testing. The ALU flag formulas are the #1 source of subtle CPU bugs in Game Boy implementations—lifting directly from gbxcule (§11.3) and generating test vectors from the same source provides defense in depth. Operand isolation (§11.2) is a power architecture decision, not an aesthetic one: it prevents large adders from seeing toggling inputs during non-ALU cycles.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone B): ALU unit tests green. Decode snapshot tests green. Single-op tests for LD/INC/DEC/ADD/AND/XOR/OR/CP green. Every opcode family in at least one test.

title: IE/IF registers in peripheral/IO layer
description:
Implement IE and IF registers in src/periph/interrupts.spade.

Registers:
- IE (0xFFFF): Interrupt Enable. Bits [4:0] enable individual interrupt sources.
- IF (0xFF0F): Interrupt Flag. Bits [4:0] indicate pending interrupts.

Interrupt sources (bit positions):
- Bit 0: VBlank
- Bit 1: LCD STAT
- Bit 2: Timer
- Bit 3: Serial
- Bit 4: Joypad

Design (§12.3):
- IE/IF live in the peripheral/IO side, NOT inside the CPU
- CPU receives a narrow pending vector: irq_pending = IE & IF (5 bits)
- CPU emits an acknowledge signal when servicing an interrupt
- On acknowledge, the corresponding IF bit is cleared

Interface to CPU:
  struct IrqPending { pending: uint<5> }  // = IE & IF, computed in peripheral layer
  struct IrqAck { ack_bit: Option<uint<3>> }  // which interrupt the CPU is acknowledging

Upper bits of IE/IF read as 1 on real hardware (bits [7:5] = 0xE0 for IF).

title: Pin Cocotb and simulator backend versions
description:
Pin Cocotb version and configure the default simulator backend.

Requirements:
- Pin Cocotb version in toolchain/python.lock
- Select and pin default simulator: Icarus Verilog for initial development, with Verilator as optional backend for long regressions (§16.4)
- Verify Cocotb can drive a trivial Spade-generated Verilog testbench
- Pin the swim (Spade build) version if not already locked
- Document simulator selection rationale in toolchain/versions.md

POLICY (§16.4): Authored dynamic testbenches are written in Python/Cocotb. If more speed is needed, switch simulator backend to Verilator. Do NOT create a separate C++/Verilator authored-test lane.

title: VRAM and OAM memory regions
description:
Implement VRAM and OAM in the bus fabric.

VRAM (0x8000-0x9FFF, 8KB):
- 0x8000-0x8FFF: tile data block 0
- 0x9000-0x97FF: tile data block 1
- 0x9800-0x9BFF: tilemap 0
- 0x9C00-0x9FFF: tilemap 1
- CPU access blocked during PPU mode 3 (reads return 0xFF, writes ignored)

OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F, 160 bytes):
- 40 entries x 4 bytes each
- Entry format: Y position, X position, tile index, attributes
- CPU access blocked during PPU mode 2 and 3
- Written by OAM DMA

Access restrictions (per BusOwner arbitration):
- Mode 2: PPU owns OAM, CPU blocked from OAM
- Mode 3: PPU owns both VRAM and OAM, CPU blocked from both
- Mode 0, 1: CPU has full access

Implementation should use the BusOwner mechanism from the bus arbitration task.

title: Hook-driven adapter for strict lockstep
description:
Implement the hook-driven adapter (§14.3) for instruction-boundary and M-cycle lockstep in bench/pyboy/hooks.py, hook_driver.py, symbols.py.

Components:
- symbols.py: Load .sym files, map symbol names to addresses
- hooks.py: Register hooks at executable labels (__pass, __fail, __checkpoint_*, __commit_*)
- hook_driver.py: Drive lockstep comparison via hook callbacks

Hook discipline (§14.4.1):
- Prefer symbol-driven hooks via .sym files over raw addresses
- Hooks work by replacing instruction at target address with special opcode
- ONLY use on deliberate executable labels, never arbitrary data/code
- Keep hook placement in one manifest owned by the harness
- Promise strict hook-driven commit comparison only for owned/instrumented ROMs
- Third-party ROMs stay at checkpoint/frame-semantic modes unless deliberately instrumented

The deep-control path is hook callbacks + snapshot/restore, NOT offline trace export (§14.3). Do NOT introduce file-based trace export as an intermediate truth source.

title: PPU mode state machine and STAT interrupt
description:
Implement PPU mode state machine in src/periph/ppu.spade.

PPU modes:
- Mode 0: HBlank (204 T-cycles typical, variable)
- Mode 1: VBlank (4560 T-cycles, lines 144-153)
- Mode 2: OAM Search (80 T-cycles)
- Mode 3: Pixel Transfer (172 T-cycles typical, variable)

Scanline timing (456 T-cycles per line):
- Lines 0-143: Mode 2 → Mode 3 → Mode 0
- Lines 144-153: Mode 1 (VBlank)

STAT register (0xFF41):
- Bits [1:0]: current mode (read-only)
- Bit 2: LY=LYC coincidence flag (read-only)
- Bit 3: Mode 0 HBlank interrupt enable
- Bit 4: Mode 1 VBlank interrupt enable
- Bit 5: Mode 2 OAM interrupt enable
- Bit 6: LY=LYC interrupt enable

STAT interrupt (IF bit 1) fires on rising edge of:
  (mode0_enable AND mode==0) OR (mode1_enable AND mode==1) OR
  (mode2_enable AND mode==2) OR (lyc_enable AND LY==LYC)

LY register (0xFF44): current scanline (0-153). Writing resets to 0.
LYC register (0xFF45): LY compare value.

title: F low nibble formal invariant
description:
Add formal verification check in formal/cpu_invariants.sby: F register low nibble is always zero.

Property:
  always (F[3:0] == 4'b0000)

This is an unconditional invariant of the SM83. The F register only uses bits [7:4] for Z, N, H, C flags. Bits [3:0] are hardwired to zero.

If this property fails, it means:
1. Some ALU operation incorrectly sets low nibble bits, OR
2. Some register write path bypasses the mask_f helper, OR
3. POP AF doesn't mask the low nibble

This should be one of the first formal checks (Phase 1 step 8). Requires SymbiYosys setup.

title: Epic: Power Optimization Pass (Phase 5)
description:
Measure, validate, and optimize the power architecture that was designed-in from Phase 2 onwards.

Three-tier measurement (§18.7):
1. Always-on logical quiescence metrics: register write-enable duty cycle, bus-active duty cycle, halt quiescence
2. Nightly activity captures: SAIF switching activity on representative ROM windows
3. Synthesis/P&R dashboard: LUT/DFF/SPRAM usage, constrained f_max, clock-enable fanout

Specific validations:
- HALT quiescence (§18.5): in HALT with no wake event, zero register writes, zero bus requests, zero ALU activity, stable phase/debug outputs
- ALU idle isolation (§18.2): when AluReq::Idle, ALU input buses stay quiet
- No debug-path contamination in hardware build (§18.6)
- Physical memory mapping (§18.7.1): WRAM/VRAM/OAM use UP5K embedded memories (SPRAM/EBR), not register arrays

Optimization candidates (measurement-driven, §18.8-18.9):
- Pipeline insertion for decode→operand→ALU→flag datapath if timing requires (§19.3)
- Encoding optimization (binary vs one-hot vs Gray) only when measurement justifies
- Clock-enable fanout optimization

RATIONALE: Thesis #3 is 'power is an architectural property, not a cleanup pass.' The power-friendly patterns (clock enables, operand isolation, sparse deltas, quiescent buses) were designed-in from Phase 2. This phase VALIDATES those decisions with actual synthesis data and ONLY optimizes where measurement proves it's needed. §18.8 explicitly warns against forcing clever encodings too early.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone E): Halt-quiescence test green. ALU idle isolation measurable. Activity baseline recorded. Synthesis baseline under explicit nextpnr clock constraints. No debug-path contamination in hardware build.

title: Epic: Repository Structure & Toolchain Setup (Phase 0)
description:
Lock down all truth sources before writing any real HDL. This epic covers:
- Creating the recommended repository directory layout (§5) with explicit CPU/bus/periph/sim/board splits
- Pinning all external dependencies: PyBoy (behavioral oracle), Cocotb (testbench framework), simulator backend (Icarus/Verilator), yosys+nextpnr-ice40 (synthesis/P&R), and formal verification tools
- Establishing the ROM manifest schema and initial custom ROM inventory
- Defining explicit model/reset/memory behavior profile enums

RATIONALE: Phase 0 exists because the architecture plan's thesis #2 is 'verification before implementation.' If truth sources drift or are ambiguous, every subsequent phase inherits that uncertainty. Pinning versions in lockfiles ensures reproducible CI and developer environments. The repo layout follows §5 to keep CPU semantic code, simulation tops, board integration, and test infrastructure cleanly separated—this separation is load-bearing for the power architecture (§18) because debug/sim code must never leak into hardware builds.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone A prerequisite): Every smoke script runs reproducibly from a clean checkout. All version pins are recorded in toolchain/ manifests.

title: Register file struct and pure accessor helpers
description:
Create src/cpu/regs.spade with the register file and all accessor functions from §9.

Register struct (§9.1):
  struct Registers {
    a: uint<8>, f: uint<8>,
    b: uint<8>, c: uint<8>,
    d: uint<8>, e: uint<8>,
    h: uint<8>, l: uint<8>,
    sp: uint<16>, pc: uint<16>,
  }

Pure accessor/update helpers (§9.2) — write once, use everywhere:
  fn get_r8(regs: Registers, sel: R8) -> uint<8>
  fn set_r8(regs: Registers, sel: R8, val: uint<8>) -> Registers
  fn get_r16(regs: Registers, pair: RegPair) -> uint<16>
  fn set_r16(regs: Registers, pair: RegPair, val: uint<16>) -> Registers
  fn flags_from_f(f: uint<8>) -> Flags
  fn pack_flags(flags: Flags) -> uint<8>

Flag struct:
  struct Flags { z: bool, n: bool, h: bool, c: bool }

F register canonical form (§9.3):
  fn mask_f(f: uint<8>) -> uint<8>  // Always zeros low nibble
  NEVER duplicate this masking ad hoc. Always use the helper.

R8/RegPair enums:
  enum R8 { A, B, C, D, E, H, L }  // Note: (HL) is NOT R8, it's a memory access
  enum RegPair { BC, DE, HL, SP, AF }

DESIGN NOTE: These are pure functions — no state, no side effects. They specify combinational semantics (§4.1 denotational layer). The operational layer (cpu_core entity) uses them but owns the actual registers.

title: ALU implementation: 8-bit logic (AND, OR, XOR)
description:
Implement 8-bit logic operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- AND A,r/n: A & operand, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=1, C=0
- OR A,r/n: A | operand, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=0, C=0
- XOR A,r/n: A ^ operand, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=0, C=0

These are the simplest ALU operations with fixed flag patterns. Good starting point for ALU verification.

title: Flag packing/unpacking helpers
description:
Implement flag helpers in src/cpu/regs.spade (or extend existing).

Functions:
  fn flags_from_f(f: uint<8>) -> Flags   // Extract Z,N,H,C from F register bits 7,6,5,4
  fn pack_flags(flags: Flags) -> uint<8>  // Pack Z,N,H,C into bits 7,6,5,4, low nibble always 0

F register canonical form (§9.3):
  fn mask_f(f: uint<8>) -> uint<8>  // f & 0xF0 — always zeros low nibble

Invariant: pack_flags(flags_from_f(f)) == mask_f(f) for all f.
The low nibble of F is ALWAYS zero on SM83. This must be enforced everywhere F is written.

These helpers are used by ALU, decoder, and CPU core. They must be pure functions.
